Name: Social Organization of Animals
ShortDescription: Pattern-level stabilization of persistent group structures through coordinated inter-animal interaction, producing reproducible collective organization beyond individual behavior.
Description: Social Organization of Animals denotes the admissible pattern-level stabilization of persistent group structures formed through sustained and structured interaction among multiple animals. Within Level 7 this arising is not a single event of coordination and not an individuated object, but a reproducible relational pattern distributed across interacting organisms through recurring association, dominance structure, cooperative allocation, synchronized movement, signaling regularities, and role differentiation. Within SOMA this arising is classified as a pattern because its identity is determined by stable organization of relations among multiple animal carriers rather than by one bounded body, isolated process episode, or single intrinsic property. This arising marks a transition from individual behavioral control to a group-level causal regime in which interaction topology constrains individual action and generates collective properties such as cohesion, hierarchy, division of roles, adaptive responsiveness, and distributed regulation. It persists despite partial turnover of individuals and operates through distributed constraint rather than centralized control.

Evidence.Reference: Wilson 1975, Sociobiology: The New Synthesis, synthesizing stable group organization, hierarchy, cooperation, and collective behavioral structure across animal species.
Krause and Ruxton 2002, Living in Groups, analyzing collective organization, grouping benefits, and persistent interaction structure in animals.
Tinbergen 1951, The Study of Instinct, foundational framework for analyzing structured social behavior and recurring interaction organization in animals.

Name: Social Roles and Stratification
ShortDescription: Pattern-level stabilization of differentiated social positions, hierarchical asymmetries, and persistent role-defined relations within animal groups.
Description: Social Roles and Stratification denotes the admissible pattern-level stabilization of differentiated positions within already organized animal groups, where recurrent interaction regularities consolidate into durable role-defined relations. Within Level 7 this arising is not a single dominance event and not an individuated object, but a reproducible relational pattern in which individuals cease to be functionally interchangeable and become associated with persistent expectations such as leadership, subordination, guarding, mediation, specialization, or preferential access. Within SOMA this arising is classified as a pattern because its identity is determined by stable organization of positional relations among multiple animal carriers rather than by one isolated process, bounded body, or intrinsic property. This arising introduces durable asymmetries in authority, access, influence, obligation, and responsibility, establishing a structured social geometry that constrains interaction pathways, regulates conflict and cooperation, and channels collective dynamics beyond the persistence of any particular individual occupant.

Evidence.Reference: Chase et al. 2002, analysis of dominance hierarchy formation in animals showing how stable rank structure emerges from repeated interaction dynamics.
Henrich and Gil-White 2001, evolutionary analysis of prestige and status differentiation relevant to persistent social asymmetry and role stabilization.
Weber 1922, Economy and Society, foundational treatment of stratification and durable social ordering as structured positional relations.

Name: Recurrent Social Practices
ShortDescription: Process-level stabilization of repeated collective action sequences within social groups, yielding durable and recognizable coordination routines.
Description: Recurrent Social Practices denotes the admissible process-level stabilization of repeated collective action sequences within already organized social groups. Within Level 7 this arising consists in temporally extended and recurrent coordination patterns through which role-differentiated agents repeatedly enact similar configurations of cooperation, conflict resolution, signaling, resource sharing, and activity cycling. Within SOMA this arising is classified as a process because its identity is defined by ordered action sequences unfolding across time rather than by a static relational pattern, bounded object, or group-level property alone. This arising marks a transition from episodic coordination to stabilized procedural continuity, where expectations become embedded directly in repeated sequences of doing rather than in explicit rules, representations, or codified instructions. Recurrent social practices reduce uncertainty, lower coordination cost, preserve continuity across participant turnover, and enable group-level functioning to remain reliable through repeated enactment.

Evidence.Reference: Bourdieu 1977, Outline of a Theory of Practice, foundational analysis of how repeated social action stabilizes into durable practical organization.
Giddens 1984, The Constitution of Society, theoretical account of recurrent social practices as the medium of ongoing social reproduction.
Schatzki 2002, The Site of the Social, conceptual treatment of practices as organized nexuses of repeated activity.

Name: Social Exchange and Resource Coordination
ShortDescription: Process-level stabilization of regulated exchange, allocation, and coordinated resource flow within social groups.
Description: Social Exchange and Resource Coordination denotes the admissible process-level stabilization of regulated mechanisms for distributing, transferring, and managing shared resources within organized social groups. Within Level 7 this arising consists in temporally extended exchange and allocation sequences such as reciprocal transfer, redistribution, obligation-based delivery, queueing, priority access, and coordinated management of common goods. Within SOMA this arising is classified as a process because its identity is defined by ordered flows of material, energetic, and access-related transfers unfolding through time rather than by one static relational pattern, bounded object, or intrinsic property. This arising marks a transition from situational cooperation to regulated resource flow, where exchanges are increasingly shaped by collective arrangements, persistent expectations, and group-level coordination structures rather than by immediate bilateral interaction alone. It enables scalability, temporal persistence, conflict reduction, buffering against local scarcity, and more stable collective metabolism within social systems.

Evidence.Reference: Polanyi 1944, The Great Transformation, analyzing redistribution, reciprocity, and coordinated resource allocation as socially organized exchange forms.
Mauss 1925, The Gift, foundational analysis of obligation-based transfer, reciprocity, and socially structured exchange.
Sahlins 1972, Stone Age Economics, conceptual treatment of exchange logics and resource coordination in non-market social systems.

Name: Collective Decision-Making
ShortDescription: Process-level group selection of actions or directions through aggregation, convergence, and stabilization of distributed social signals.
Description: Collective Decision-Making denotes the admissible process-level organization by which socially organized groups aggregate distributed signals, preferences, constraints, or action tendencies into a single group-level outcome. Within Level 7 this arising consists in temporally extended mechanisms such as consensus formation, quorum dynamics, majority convergence, leader-mediated selection, and distributed decision cascades through which multiple local inputs are transformed into one stabilized collective choice. Within SOMA this arising is classified as a process because its identity is defined by ordered aggregation, convergence, threshold transition, and outcome stabilization unfolding through time rather than by a static group pattern, bounded object, or intrinsic property. The resulting decision is not attributable to any single individual but is produced by interaction topology, information flow, asymmetries of influence, and coordination procedures embedded in the group. This arising establishes a causal regime in which group-level choice reorganizes subsequent individual behavior, future coordination pathways, and shared collective action.

Evidence.Reference: Conradt and Roper 2005, analysis of consensus decision making in animals showing how group-level choices emerge from distributed interaction.
Couzin et al. 2011, evidence that uninformed individuals can promote democratic consensus through interaction dynamics in collective choice.
Arrow 1951, Social Choice and Individual Values, foundational conceptual treatment of aggregation from individual inputs to collective outcomes.

Name: Structures of Human Society
ShortDescription: Pattern-level persistent large-scale social organization structuring human populations beyond immediate groups through durable positional and coordinative arrangements.
Description: Structures of Human Society denotes the admissible pattern-level stabilization of persistent large-scale social organization extending beyond immediate face-to-face groups. Within Level 7 this arising is not a single coordinated process and not one bounded collective object, but a reproducible relational pattern spanning populations through stratified layers, kinship and descent arrangements, territorial ordering, authority distributions, and durable frameworks of coordination maintained across generations. Within SOMA this arising is classified as a pattern because its identity is determined by stable organization of relations among many human carriers and subgroups rather than by one isolated action sequence, institution, or intrinsic property. This arising functions as an abstract social ordering principle that constrains individual and group behavior independently of specific participants, enabling continuity, scalability, institutional persistence, intergenerational transmission, and cumulative social complexity. It marks the stabilization of society-level structure as a distinct organizational regime beyond local interaction networks.

Evidence.Reference: Durkheim 1895, The Rules of Sociological Method, establishing society-level social facts as durable structures constraining individual behavior.
Parsons 1951, The Social System, synthesizing persistent structural arrangements that organize roles, norms, and large-scale social coordination.
Giddens 1984, The Constitution of Society, conceptual treatment of durable social structuration across time and population turnover.

Name: Collective Intelligence under Distributed Aggregation
ShortDescription: Property-level group cognitive capacity in which distributed populations integrate partially independent contributions into judgments or solutions that can outperform typical individual performance under suitable aggregation conditions.
Description: Collective Intelligence under Distributed Aggregation denotes the admissible property-level cognitive capacity of socially structured populations to transform distributed individual signals into reliable group-level judgments, solutions, forecasts, or problem-solving outcomes when diversity of perspective, partial independence, structured aggregation, and network-level coordination jointly support improved inference under uncertainty. This arising is not identical to one voting rule or one communication format. It consists in a stable capacity attributable to the organized collective when error cancellation, distributed exploration of solution spaces, selective signal integration, confidence-weighting, redundancy reduction, and aggregation architecture together allow the group as a whole to infer, estimate, or solve more effectively than typical isolated participants. Wisdom-of-crowds effects form one important special case of this broader capacity, but the arising also includes more interactive forms of collective problem solving in which coordination, role differentiation, and iterative exchange alter group-level epistemic performance. Within SOMA this is classified as a property because its identity lies in a capability attributable to the organized collective system rather than in one isolated decision process, bounded institutional object, or static interaction pattern alone.

Evidence.Reference: Surowiecki 2004, The Wisdom of Crowds, synthesizing conditions under which distributed groups outperform typical individual judgment.
Woolley et al. 2010, evidence for a collective intelligence factor in human groups, showing group-level performance variation not reducible to individual IQ alone.
Page 2007, The Difference, conceptual analysis of how diversity improves collective problem-solving and inference.

Name: Superorganism Behavior in Insect Colonies
ShortDescription: Process-level colony-wide adaptive behavior emerging from distributed coordination among caste-differentiated insects functioning as an integrated superorganism.
Description: Superorganism Behavior in Insect Colonies denotes the admissible process-level organization in which large populations of relatively simple insects coordinate through local interactions to generate coherent adaptive behavior at the colony level. Within Level 7 this arising consists in temporally extended colony-wide processes such as foraging, nest construction, brood care, defense, reproduction, environmental regulation, and dynamic task reallocation under changing internal and external conditions. Within SOMA this arising is classified as a process because its identity is defined by ordered and ongoing colony-level behavioral coordination unfolding through time rather than by a static relational pattern, bounded collective object, or global property alone. The colony thereby functions as an integrated higher-order behavioral unit in which caste differentiation, pheromonal signaling, contact-based interactions, feedback-regulated task allocation, and homeostatic adjustment jointly sustain adaptive collective performance. This arising persists independently of individual identities and is irreducible to single-organism control, establishing colony-level behavior as a distinct organizational regime.

Evidence.Reference: Hölldobler and Wilson 2009, The Superorganism, synthesizing colony-level coordination, caste organization, and distributed adaptive behavior in social insects.
Gordon 2010, Ant Encounters, analyzing how local interactions generate colony-level behavioral regulation without centralized control.
Wheeler 1911, The ant-colony as an organism, foundational conceptualization of insect colonies as integrated higher-order behavioral units.

Name: Social Norms and Conventions
ShortDescription: Property-level shared expectation structure regulating behavior through distributed social feedback, mutual monitoring, and informal sanction dynamics.
Description: Social Norms and Conventions denotes the admissible property-level emergence of shared expectation structures that regulate behavior within socially organized groups. Within Level 7 this arising is expressed as a system-level regulatory condition in which appropriate and inappropriate behavior become collectively anticipated through recurrent interaction, mutual monitoring, reputation effects, approval, disapproval, and exclusion dynamics. Within SOMA this arising is classified as a property because its identity lies in a persistent regulatory condition attributable to the social system rather than in one isolated action sequence, bounded collective object, or static positional pattern. These norms and conventions operate without centralized enforcement, embedding behavioral constraints directly into the expectation architecture of the group and thereby reducing coordination costs, stabilizing interaction under uncertainty, and enabling cooperation across larger social scales. This arising formalizes informal social regulation as a distinct collective property of the system.

Evidence.Reference: Bicchieri 2006, The Grammar of Society, analyzing norms as shared expectations sustained by conditional preferences and social feedback.
Elster 1989, The Cement of Society, examining how social norms regulate behavior through informal sanction and mutual expectation.
Lewis 1969, Convention, foundational account of convention as coordination stabilized by common expectation.

Name: Dynamics of Economic Markets
ShortDescription: Process-level decentralized coordination of prices, allocation, and production-consumption flows emerging through large-scale social exchange and feedback.
Description: Dynamics of Economic Markets denotes the admissible process-level emergence of decentralized price formation, allocation, and coordination of production and consumption through large-scale social exchange. Within Level 7 this arising consists in temporally extended transactional and informational processes through which distributed local decisions aggregate into global patterns such as prices, volumes, volatility, cycles, shortages, surpluses, bubbles, and crashes. Within SOMA this arising is classified as a process because its identity is defined by ongoing ordered exchanges, adjustments, feedback loops, and regime transitions unfolding through time rather than by a static institutional pattern, bounded collective object, or single system property. This arising constitutes a social regulatory regime that coordinates resources without centralized control, relying on distributed interaction, expectation formation, signal propagation, trust structures, and adaptive response to changing conditions. It exhibits nonlinearity, path dependence, episodic instability, and emergent large-scale coordination irreducible to isolated individual decisions.

Evidence.Reference: Smith 1776, An Inquiry into the Nature and Causes of the Wealth of Nations, foundational analysis of decentralized exchange and market coordination.
Arthur 2015, Complexity and the Economy, synthesizing market dynamics as nonlinear interaction-driven complex systems.
Hayek 1945, The Use of Knowledge in Society, conceptual account of how dispersed information becomes coordinated through market signals.

Name: Political and Governance Structures
ShortDescription: Object-level institutional configurations organizing collective rule-setting, authority, legitimacy, and enforcement across large-scale populations.
Description: Political and Governance Structures denotes the admissible object-level formation of durable institutional configurations through which large-scale social systems define, enforce, and revise binding decisions across heterogeneous populations. Within Level 7 this arising is treated as an object because it consists in identifiable organized social formations composed of offices, roles, procedures, decision channels, legitimacy structures, and enforcement capacities that persist as bounded institutional units across episodes of participation and personnel turnover. Within SOMA this arising is not merely a transient governance process or a diffuse social property, but a persistent organized configuration through which authority becomes structurally embodied and socially operative. These structures formalize collective decision-making, codify legitimacy and compliance expectations, establish enforceable jurisdiction, and enable long-horizon coordination under scale, conflict, and complexity. They constrain behavior, regulate disputes, stabilize rule application, and maintain collective order beyond informal norms or voluntary exchange alone.

Evidence.Reference: North 1990, Institutions, Institutional Change and Economic Performance, analyzing durable institutional structures that shape incentives, compliance, and collective coordination.
Ostrom 1990, Governing the Commons, demonstrating structured governance arrangements for rule formation, monitoring, and enforcement in collective systems.
Weber 1919, Politics as a Vocation, conceptual treatment of political authority, legitimacy, and organized domination as durable governance formations.

Name: Evolution of Complex Social Institutions
ShortDescription: Object-level historically extended institutional formations that reproduce, stabilize, and transform large-scale social coordination across generations.
Description: Evolution of Complex Social Institutions denotes the admissible object-level formation of historically extended institutional systems through which governance structures, social norms, and recurrent practices consolidate into formally defined and reproductively persistent social objects. Within Level 7 this arising is treated as an object because institutions such as law, education, religion, science, and finance exist as identifiable organized formations with bounded rule systems, role hierarchies, enforcement channels, reproduction procedures, and reform pathways that persist across episodes of participation and across generations. Within SOMA this arising is not merely a governance process or a diffuse social property, but a durable institutional configuration capable of cumulative modification, path dependence, inertia, and selective adaptation under changing historical conditions. These institutions exert downward constraints on both individual and collective behavior while remaining sensitive to long-term environmental, economic, political, and cultural pressures. This arising marks a threshold at which social structures become historically extended objects with internal continuity and autonomous causal efficacy.

Evidence.Reference: North 1990, Institutions, Institutional Change and Economic Performance, analyzing long-term persistence and transformation of formal institutions under path dependence and incentive structure.
Acemoglu and Robinson 2012, Why Nations Fail, examining how institutional configurations shape long-term political and economic trajectories across societies.
March and Olsen 1989, Rediscovering Institutions, conceptual treatment of institutions as enduring organized structures with autonomous effects on political and social behavior.

Name: Cultural and Linguistic Evolutionary Change
ShortDescription: Pattern-level cumulative symbolic lineage formation through social transmission, variation, stabilization, and intergenerational reproduction.
Description: Cultural and Linguistic Evolutionary Change denotes the admissible process-level transformation through which symbolic systems, linguistic structures, shared practices, meanings, and transmissible cultural forms undergo long-duration variation, selection, retention, recombination, and historical reorganization across populations and generations. This arising is not a single communicative act and not one bounded symbolic object. It consists in extended trajectories of change through which vocabularies, grammatical conventions, semantic fields, ritual forms, interpretive repertoires, and transmitted cultural structures are preserved, modified, stratified, hybridized, or displaced under conditions of social transmission, institutional stabilization, conflict, prestige bias, and cross-group interaction. The arising therefore captures broad historical-cultural transformation rather than any one medium or one propagating symbolic unit, establishing a temporally extended evolutionary regime of shared meaning structures across societies.

Evidence.Reference: Boyd and Richerson 1985, Culture and the Evolutionary Process, analyzing cultural transmission, variation, and cumulative adaptation as a distinct evolutionary regime.
Pagel 2009, Human language as a culturally transmitted replicator, examining linguistic change as lineage-forming cultural evolution.
Tomasello 1999, The Cultural Origins of Human Cognition, conceptual account of shared intentionality and symbolic transmission underlying cumulative culture.

Name: Morphogenesis of Cities and Urban Dynamics
ShortDescription: Pattern-level socio-spatial organization of dense settlements emerging from coupled social, economic, infrastructural, and regulatory interactions.
Description: Morphogenesis of Cities and Urban Dynamics denote the admissible pattern-level formation of large-scale socio-spatial organization in which dense and persistent settlements emerge from coupled interactions among social structure, economic exchange, governance, mobility, infrastructure, and cultural practice. This arising is not a single planning process and not one bounded institutional object, but a reproducible relational pattern distributed across territory through density gradients, transport corridors, land-use differentiation, neighborhood segregation, agglomeration structure, infrastructural layering, and feedback between built form and social function. Within SOMA this arising is classified as a pattern because its identity is determined by stable organization of spatial relations, flow structures, and heterogeneous urban subregions across many human carriers and institutions rather than by one isolated action sequence or singular governing body. Cities thereby operate as self-organizing socio-spatial systems exhibiting path dependence, uneven concentration of opportunity, infrastructural inertia, and reciprocal shaping between collective coordination and material layout. This arising establishes an autonomous urban pattern regime with its own constraints, attractors, and long-duration developmental trajectories.

Evidence.Reference: Batty 2013, The New Science of Cities, synthesizing cities as complex socio-spatial systems with emergent morphology and dynamic feedback structure.
Bettencourt et al. 2007, analysis of growth, innovation, scaling, and pace-of-life regularities demonstrating reproducible urban system laws.
Jacobs 1961, The Death and Life of Great American Cities, conceptual treatment of urban form as emerging from dense interaction, diversity, and everyday spatial practice.

Name: Digital Social Networks
ShortDescription: Pattern-level socio-technical interaction graphs in which digital mediation, persistent traces, and algorithmic filtering reshape collective social dynamics at scale.
Description: Digital Social Networks denote the admissible pattern-level formation of large-scale socio-technical interaction graphs in which social communication, coordination, reputation management, and information exchange become persistently mediated by digital platforms and their associated ranking, recommendation, and trace-retention architectures. This arising is not a single communicative process and not one bounded platform object, but a reproducible relational pattern distributed across users, interaction histories, content traces, algorithmic curation layers, and platform-mediated feedback channels. Its identity lies in stable organization of interaction topology jointly with automated visibility management rather than in one isolated action sequence, institutional object, or social property alone. Through recommendation systems, selective amplification, trace persistence, and high-speed diffusion, these networks reshape influence distribution, collective attention, polarization dynamics, reputational accumulation, coordination thresholds, and the temporal persistence of social signals. The arising therefore formalizes a distinct socio-technical regime in which digital mediation and social structure jointly constrain large-scale collective behavior independently of any single participant's intent.

Evidence.Reference: Boyd and Ellison 2007, Social network sites: Definition, history, and scholarship, foundational synthesis of digitally mediated social graph formation and persistence.
Barabási 2016, Network Science, analyzing large-scale network topology, diffusion, clustering, and structural dynamics relevant to digital social systems.
Castells 2009, Communication Power, conceptual treatment of digitally mediated communication networks as power-shaping socio-technical structures.

Name: Formation of Influence Networks
ShortDescription: Pattern-level stabilization of asymmetric social graph structures that distribute influence, visibility, credibility, and coordination leverage unevenly across populations.
Description: Formation of Influence Networks denotes the admissible pattern-level stabilization of asymmetric relational structures through which influence, visibility, credibility, trust, and coordination leverage become unevenly distributed across a social system. This arising is not a single persuasive event and not one bounded institutional object, but a reproducible mesoscopic pattern in which repeated interaction, reputational feedback, selective attention, mediated amplification, and differential access to audiences generate persistent who-affects-whom asymmetries. Within SOMA this arising is classified as a pattern because its identity is determined by stable organization of weighted and directed influence relations among many social carriers rather than by one isolated process, property, or formal institution. These networks channel information flow, bias opinion formation, shape agenda priority, and concentrate amplification power in specific nodes, clusters, or bridging positions across informal leadership settings, expert communities, media ecosystems, and digital platforms. The arising therefore establishes a distinct mesoscopic layer mediating between individual agency, collective cognition, and institutional authority.

Evidence.Reference: Katz and Lazarsfeld 1955, Personal Influence, foundational analysis of asymmetric influence transmission in social networks and opinion formation.
Watts and Dodds 2007, analysis of influentials, network topology, and public opinion formation showing distributed and topology-dependent influence dynamics.
Burt 1992, Structural Holes, conceptual treatment of network position, brokerage, and asymmetric influence advantage.

Name: Epistemic Markets and Knowledge Valuation
ShortDescription: Process-level distributed allocation of attention, credibility, interpretive weight, and epistemic value across information and knowledge systems.
Description: Epistemic Markets and Knowledge Valuation denote the admissible process-level organization through which socially distributed systems allocate attention, credibility, interpretive weight, legitimacy, and symbolic value to informational artifacts such as claims, data, explanations, expertise, models, and conceptual frameworks. This arising consists in temporally extended processes of evaluation, endorsement, citation, ranking, reputational amplification, diffusion, contestation, recombination, and obsolescence through which informational artifacts gain or lose epistemic standing. It is not reducible to one platform or one institutional setting, but spans scientific, media, educational, policy, and digitally mediated publics in which informational selection is shaped by trust, prestige, interpretive consensus, visibility, and network effects rather than by direct exchange of physical goods. The arising therefore formalizes a distributed valuation regime that determines which ideas persist, stabilize, circulate, recombine, or decay, thereby shaping collective understanding, innovation trajectories, and epistemic stability without requiring centralized control.

Evidence.Reference: Merton 1973, The Sociology of Science, analyzing reputational structure, recognition dynamics, and normative organization in knowledge-producing communities.
Shapin 1995, A Social History of Truth, examining how credibility, trust, and social validation govern acceptance of knowledge claims.
Polanyi 1966, The Tacit Dimension, conceptual account of epistemic judgment, expertise, and non-explicit components of knowledge evaluation.

Name: Cooperation and Conflict in Large-Scale Societies
ShortDescription: Process-level co-organization of large-scale cooperation and structured conflict across populations exceeding direct interpersonal coordination.
Description: Cooperation and Conflict in Large-Scale Societies denotes the admissible process-level co-organization by which mechanisms of exchange, governance, norms, identity, and information flow interact across populations that exceed the limits of direct interpersonal coordination. Within Level 7 this arising consists in temporally extended processes of alliance formation, collective goods provision, regulated competition, institutionalized rivalry, coordinated mobilization, dispute channeling, and conflict containment or escalation across large-scale social systems. Within SOMA this arising is classified as a process because its identity is defined by ongoing ordered dynamics of coordination, opposition, stabilization, and transformation unfolding through time rather than by a static social pattern, bounded institutional object, or collective property alone. Cooperation and conflict are treated here as co-evolving modes of regulation rather than simple opposites, since conflict can become structured, channeled, and institutionally organized while cooperation can depend on exclusion, competition, or rivalry boundaries. This arising shapes alliance architecture, institutional design, stability regimes, and large-scale social transformation by regulating how populations coordinate, compete, and reorganize under shared pressures.

Evidence.Reference: Tilly 2003, The Politics of Collective Violence, analyzing how large-scale conflict becomes organized, collective, and politically structured rather than merely episodic.
Axelrod 1984, The Evolution of Cooperation, foundational analysis of how cooperation can stabilize under repeated interaction and strategic interdependence.
Coser 1956, The Functions of Social Conflict, conceptual treatment of conflict as an organizing and structuring force within social systems.

Name: Cultural Transmission, Recombination, and Idea Propagation
ShortDescription: Pattern-level propagation of socially transmitted symbolic variants that spread, mutate, recombine, and compete across populations and media.
Description: Cultural Transmission, Recombination, and Idea Propagation denote the admissible pattern-level formation of socially transmitted symbolic variants that spread across individuals, institutions, media systems, and networked publics through repeated communication, imitation, reinterpretation, and selective retention. This arising is not a single communication event and not one bounded symbolic object, but a reproducible evolutionary pattern in which ideas, narratives, slogans, images, frames, and other symbolic constructs undergo branching, transformation, recombination, amplification, and extinction across time. Within SOMA this arising is classified as a pattern because its identity is determined by stable organization of transmission, variation, selection, and persistence relations among symbolic carriers rather than by one isolated process episode, institutional object, or collective property alone. These transmitted variants shape attention, belief formation, coordination, and behavior through salience, affective resonance, social prestige, network topology, and contextual fit rather than through factual accuracy alone. The arising therefore establishes a distinct cultural-evolutionary regime embedded in social systems, characterized by competitive diffusion, recurrent mutation, selective persistence, and large-scale propagation dynamics.

Evidence.Reference: Dawkins 1976, The Selfish Gene, introducing memes as replicating cultural units subject to variation and selection.
Blackmore 1999, The Meme Machine, synthesizing memetic transmission, selection, and cultural replication dynamics.
Shifman 2014, Memes in Digital Culture, analyzing memetic variation, diffusion, and recombination in digitally mediated environments.

Name: Social Identity and Group Belonging
ShortDescription: Property-level stabilization of group affiliation structures that shape loyalty, boundary formation, trust, and intergroup differentiation.
Description: Social Identity and Group Belonging denotes the admissible property-level stabilization of collective affiliation structures through which in-group and out-group membership become socially and psychologically operative across populations. Within Level 7 this arising is expressed as a system-level social property in which shared norms, symbols, narratives, and feedback loops organize durable categories of belonging that individuals internalize as part of self-conception and group alignment. Within SOMA this arising is classified as a property because its identity lies in a persistent collective-affiliative condition attributable to the social system rather than in one isolated interaction process, bounded institutional object, or static network pattern alone. This arising embeds emotional, moral, and behavioral dispositions into identity structures, shaping perception, trust, cooperation, exclusion, and conflict independently of immediate incentives or explicit rules. At larger scales it amplifies internal coordination, stabilizes symbolic boundaries, and structures rivalry, alliance formation, and differentiation among groups.

Evidence.Reference: Tajfel and Turner 1979, integrative theory of intergroup conflict explaining how social categorization and group identity shape cooperation and antagonism.
Brewer 1991, analysis of the social self and the simultaneous need for inclusion and differentiation in group identity formation.
Anderson 1983, Imagined Communities, conceptual treatment of large-scale collective belonging sustained through shared symbolic frameworks.

Name: Societal Feedback on Individual Neurodynamics
ShortDescription: Property-level downward and diagonal modulation of individual neural regimes by social structures, identities, norms, and collective states.
Description: Societal Feedback on Individual Neurodynamics denotes the admissible property-level cross-level modulation through which stabilized social structures, shared identities, institutions, norms, and collective affective environments systematically bias neural activity, plasticity, stress responsivity, valuation, attentional allocation, and functional regimes within individuals. This arising is expressed as a socially induced regulatory condition in which higher-level social organization becomes neurally operative through repeated symbolic exposure, reputational pressure, normative expectations, social evaluation, chronic stressors, role obligations, and institutionalized reward–penalty structures. It does not depend on one isolated interpersonal episode, but on persistent embedding of individuals within structured social environments whose patterns are repeatedly internalized through learning, affective conditioning, and state regulation. The arising therefore formalizes downward and diagonal causation from social organization to neural organization, constraining learning sensitivity, affective baseline, cognitive salience, and decision thresholds without requiring direct genetic or pharmacological intervention. Within SOMA this is a property because it characterizes a stable cross-level conditioning state attributable to the social–individual system rather than to one transient event alone.

Evidence.Reference: Cacioppo and Hawkley 2009, analysis of perceived social isolation and its effects on cognition, neural processing, and regulatory function.
Eisenberger and Cole 2012, synthesis in social neuroscience and health showing pathways by which social conditions modulate neural and physiological regulation.
Bourdieu 1990, The Logic of Practice, conceptual framework for how enduring social structures become embodied in dispositions and patterned response tendencies.

Name: Sociogenesis of Shared Information Environments
ShortDescription: Pattern-level formation of shared societal information environments that organize salience, credibility, interpretation, and coordination across large populations.
Description: Sociogenesis of Shared Information Environments denotes the admissible pattern-level formation of shared societal-scale informational environments produced by the integration of information flows, symbolic representations, influence networks, media infrastructures, and collective attention. This arising is not a single communication process and not one bounded media object, but a reproducible relational pattern distributed across populations through attention gradients, amplification hubs, credibility asymmetries, discourse pathways, memetic circulation, and persistent interpretive frames. Within SOMA this arising is classified as a pattern because its identity is determined by stable organization of informational relations and salience structures across many social carriers rather than by one isolated process episode, institutional object, or collective property alone. These shared information environments shape what becomes salient, discussable, credible, ignored, amplified, or suppressed across society, thereby guiding coordination, expectation, and interpretation without requiring centralized control. They function as distributed societal attractor structures with inertia, tipping points, and path dependence, modulating discourse and action across time and space.

Evidence.Reference: Luhmann 1995, Social Systems, theoretical treatment of society-wide communication structures as self-reproducing informational environments.
McCombs and Shaw 1972, analysis of agenda-setting showing how media environments shape collective salience and public attention.
Castells 2010, The Rise of the Network Society, conceptual account of networked informational environments organizing social meaning and coordination at scale.

Name: Transculturation and Hybridization of Traditions
ShortDescription: Pattern-level formation of hybrid cultural configurations through sustained interaction, recombination, and restabilization of distinct traditions.
Description: Transculturation and Hybridization of Traditions denotes the admissible pattern-level formation of hybrid cultural configurations produced through sustained interaction among distinct cultural systems. Within Level 7 this arising is not a single exchange event and not one bounded institutional object, but a reproducible large-scale pattern in which symbols, practices, narratives, identities, and interpretive forms are selectively recombined, recontextualized, and restabilized into novel cultural configurations. Within SOMA this arising is classified as a pattern because its identity is determined by stable organization of recombined cultural relations and transmission structures across populations and time rather than by one isolated process episode, collective property, or source tradition alone. This arising formalizes reciprocal transformation rather than unidirectional diffusion or simple assimilation, generating hybrid traditions that are not reducible to their cultural sources. It enables adaptation to new social, technological, and geopolitical conditions while reshaping collective memory, meaning, and identity at population scale.

Evidence.Reference: Ortiz 1940, Cuban Counterpoint: Tobacco and Sugar, foundational analysis of transculturation as reciprocal cultural transformation rather than one-way assimilation.
Hannerz 1996, Transnational Connections, analyzing cross-cultural interaction, recombination, and hybridization in interconnected societies.
Appadurai 1996, Modernity at Large, conceptual treatment of global cultural flows, disjunctures, and hybridization of symbolic forms.

Name: Mass Cognitive Distortion Regimes
ShortDescription: Property-level societal distortion regimes in perception, judgment, and belief formation produced by synchronized cognitive and informational reinforcement.
Description: Mass Cognitive Distortion Regimes denote the admissible property-level emergence of systematic collective distortion in perception, judgment, attribution, and belief formation at societal scale. This arising is expressed as a population-level epistemic condition in which individual heuristics, bounded rationality, interpretive shortcuts, and emotionally loaded salience cues become synchronized, reinforced, and amplified through social interaction, media environments, identity alignment, idea propagation, and shared information environments. It is not reducible to isolated individual error. Instead, it characterizes a stable distortion tendency attributable to the coupled social–informational–cognitive system, in which information topology, affective resonance, repeated framing, selective amplification, and group alignment jointly bias evidence evaluation, causal attribution, risk perception, and belief updating. The arising therefore formalizes how large populations can enter persistent miscalibrated interpretive regimes that shape discourse, collective behavior, and policy outcomes independently of any single individual's intent.

Evidence.Reference: Tversky and Kahneman 1974, Judgment under uncertainty, foundational analysis of heuristics and systematic bias in human judgment.
Sunstein 2002, analysis of group polarization showing how social interaction amplifies aligned biases and extremity in collective belief formation.
Kahneman 2011, Thinking, Fast and Slow, conceptual synthesis of cognitive shortcuts, misjudgment patterns, and their broad behavioral consequences.

Name: Global Governance and Supranational Institutions
ShortDescription: Object-level transnational coordination structures organizing collective rule-setting, compliance, and regulation beyond individual states.
Description: Global Governance and Supranational Institutions denote the admissible object-level formation of transnational coordination structures through which political authority, regulatory frameworks, collective rule-setting, monitoring, and coordinated action extend beyond individual states. This arising is treated as an object because it consists in identifiable organized formations such as treaties, multilateral organizations, international regimes, supranational bodies, and persistent transnational compliance architectures with bounded procedures, roles, decision channels, enforcement asymmetries, and coordination capacities that persist across changing participants and historical episodes. It is not merely a recurring coordination process or a diffuse global property. Rather, it forms a durable organized configuration that acquires non-sovereign yet causally effective authority across heterogeneous actors, linking states, firms, publics, and expert systems through partially institutionalized global rule structures. The arising therefore establishes a distinct global coordination object with path dependence, adaptive capacity, legitimacy conflicts, and causal influence irreducible to bilateral diplomacy or domestic governance alone.

Evidence.Reference: Keohane and Nye 2001, Power and Interdependence, analyzing transnational coordination under complex interdependence beyond state-centered sovereignty.
Held et al. 1999, Global Transformations, synthesizing the emergence of global governance structures under intensified interconnectedness and shared risk.
Ruggie 1982, International regimes, transactions, and change, conceptual treatment of international regimes as durable coordinated structures beyond bilateral interaction.
